label: 48_world order_international order_powers_liberal
description: The liberal world order established after World War II faces threats from rising great powers like Russia and China. Critics argue that the U.S. must act to maintain stability, while others warn of potential collapse if it does not.

earliest_articles.title: Come Together
earliest_articles.excerpt: In 2009, the Obama administration tried to patch up America’s relations with the world. In 2010, it may have to make hard choices about who it really trusts. The United States has gone out of its way to accommodate rising powers like India, Brazil and – above all – China, giving them a place at the top table in…

earliest_articles.title: Does Fairness Matter?
earliest_articles.excerpt: Introduction Worldwide, there has been a recent increase in expressions of cynicism. We are reminded that all power is hard power, and that being loved or respected is no substitute for being feared. The great power game of nations always continues, we are forewarned, even when a higher goal or rhetoric is evoked.…

earliest_articles.title: Around the Halls: A New National Security Strategy
earliest_articles.excerpt: Secretary of State Hillary Clinton visited Brookings on May 27 to discuss the Obama administration’s new National Security Strategy. Experts from around the halls of Brookings discuss the new strategy and its implications for nuclear proliferation, terrorism and al Qaeda, the use of military force, development and…

newest_articles.title: Peril and possibility: Collapsing old order, emerging disorder, or new order?
newest_articles.excerpt: This essay is adapted from a speech Fiona Hill gave for the World Traders Annual Tacitus Lecture at The Guildhall in London on February 26, 2026. When we hear phrases like “collapsing old order,” “emerging disorder,” and “new order,” they sound like three successive acts in the drama of global governance. The “old…

newest_articles.title: Is Trump reshaping the world order?
newest_articles.excerpt: President Donald Trump’s recent foreign policy moves have sent shockwaves across the international community. Brookings scholars examine how these actions are disrupting long-established norms—and what that means for the United States and the international order. We built the order for a reason President Donald Trump…

central_articles.title: The world America made—and Trump wants to unmake
central_articles.excerpt: The U.S.-led global order created peace and prosperity for millions, writes Robert Kagan. So why are the president’s critics teaming up with him to tear it apart? This piece originally appeared in Politico Magazine. The liberal world order is taking a beating these days, and not just at the hands of Donald Trump,…

central_articles.title: The twilight of the liberal world order
central_articles.excerpt: The liberal world order established in the aftermath of World War II may be coming to an end, challenged by forces both without and within. The external challenges come from the ambition of dissatisfied large and medium-size powers to overturn the existing strategic order dominated by the United States and its allies…

central_articles.title: Backing into World War III
central_articles.excerpt: Americans tend to take the fundamental stability of the international order for granted, but history shows that world orders do collapse, writes Robert Kagan. At this stage, the United States must check the assertive, rising powers of Russia and China before it’s too late, rather than accept spheres of influence. This…
